title: Ramesh Kumar Sharma v. Hindustan Polymers Ltd. & Anr.
keyArguments: The termination of the petitioner was carried out without following the principles of natural justice as no proper show cause notice was served nor was an opportunity of hearing provided before passing the termination order.
The domestic inquiry conducted by the respondent company was procedurally flawed, biased, and the inquiry officer had a conflict of interest, rendering the findings of the inquiry null and void.

The petitioner had completed more than 10 years of continuous service and was entitled to protection under the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947, and the termination amounted to retrenchment without payment of retrenchment compensation.
The respondent failed to produce any cogent evidence to substantiate allegations of misconduct against the petitioner, and the charges were vague and unsupported by documentary proof.
judgmentSummary: The Delhi High Court allowed the writ petition filed by the employee Ramesh Kumar Sharma challenging his termination from service by Hindustan Polymers Ltd. The Court held that the domestic inquiry was vitiated by procedural irregularities and violation of natural justice principles. The termination order was quashed and the petitioner was directed to be reinstated with 50% back wages from the date of termination.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI
W.P.(C) No. 11342/2022
Date of Decision: 14th March, 2023

RAMESH KUMAR SHARMA ... Petitioner
versus
HINDUSTAN POLYMERS LTD. & ANR. ... Respondents

CORAM: HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE ANIL KUMAR SRIVASTAVA

JUDGMENT

1. The present writ petition has been filed by the petitioner under Article 226 of the Constitution of India challenging the termination order dated 17th August, 2021 passed by Respondent No. 1, Hindustan Polymers Ltd., and praying for reinstatement with full back wages and continuity of service.

2. The petitioner was employed as a Senior Accounts Executive with the respondent company since 12th January, 2011. On 03rd March, 2021, a charge sheet was issued against him alleging financial irregularities and misappropriation of company funds amounting to Rs. 2,14,000/-. The petitioner submitted his reply to the charge sheet on 18th March, 2021. Thereafter, a domestic inquiry was initiated and the inquiry officer submitted his report dated 10th July, 2021, finding the petitioner guilty of the charges. Consequentially, the impugned termination order was passed on 17th August, 2021.

3. Learned counsel for the petitioner vehemently argued that the entire domestic inquiry proceeding was a sham exercise. The inquiry officer, one Mr. D.K. Malhotra, was the direct superior of the petitioner and had personal animosity towards him, which constitutes a clear bias vitiating the inquiry ab initio. Further, the petitioner was not given adequate opportunity to cross-examine the witnesses produced by the management, and several documents relied upon in the inquiry report were never supplied to the petitioner during the course of inquiry.

4. Learned counsel for the respondents submitted that the inquiry was conducted in accordance with the Standing Orders applicable to the establishment and the petitioner was afforded full opportunity to present his defence. It was further urged that the findings of the inquiry officer are based on evidence on record and this Court should not interfere in the quantum of punishment.

5. This Court has carefully perused the inquiry record and proceedings. It is evident from the record that the inquiry officer, Mr. D.K. Malhotra, was indeed a reporting authority of the petitioner, which created a reasonable apprehension of bias. The Supreme Court in Ram Chandra Singh v. Savitri Devi, (2003) 8 SCC 319, has clearly held that an inquiry conducted by a biased officer cannot form the basis of punishment. Furthermore, this Court notes that the petitioner's written request dated 22nd April, 2021 for supply of documents relied upon in the inquiry was never acceded to, which is a fundamental violation of the principles of natural justice as enunciated in Union of India v. T.R. Varma, AIR 1957 SC 882.

6. On the question of charges themselves, the respondent company has failed to produce any primary documentary evidence to establish misappropriation. The allegations are based entirely on the testimony of interested witnesses who are employees of the respondent company without any independent corroboration. The standard of proof in departmental proceedings, while not as stringent as in criminal trials, still requires that the charges be proved by preponderance of evidence based on reliable material, as held in Nirmala J. Jhala v. State of Gujarat, (2013) 4 SCC 301.

7. In view of the foregoing analysis, this Court is of the considered opinion that the termination of the petitioner was illegal and unsustainable in law. The termination order dated 17th August, 2021 is hereby quashed and set aside. The respondent company is directed to reinstate the petitioner in service within a period of four weeks from the date of this judgment. The petitioner shall be entitled to 50% back wages from the date of termination till the date of actual reinstatement. Continuity of service for all consequential benefits shall also be maintained.

8. The writ petition is accordingly allowed. Parties shall bear their own costs.

(ANIL KUMAR SRIVASTAVA)
JUDGE
HIGH COURT OF DELHI
14th March, 2023
